high_value_elements: Correctly distinguishes acute pain scales (used in-hospital by trained staff) from chronic CMIs (owner-completed, measuring function over days to weeks)

Feline acute scales named with thresholds: (1) Feline Grimace Scale — 5 action units (ear positioning, orbital tightening, muzzle tension, whisker position, head position), max 10, threshold ≥4; (2) UF EPS Short Form — max 12, threshold ≤4; (3) Glasgow Composite Skin Pain Scale for cats — max 20, threshold ≥5
Canine acute scale named: Glasgow CMPS-SF — 6 items, max 24 (mobile) or 20 (immobile), rescue threshold ≥6 (mobile) or ≥5 (immobile)
Canine chronic CMIs named: CBPI — 11 items, 3 domains (pain severity, pain interference with function, quality of life); LOAD — 13 items, 2 mobility subscales; COAST for staging
Correctly describes FAS: Fear, Anxiety, Stress; elevated FAS score means pain scale scores are unreliable; intervention (anxiolytic medication, environmental modification) required before re-scoring
major_concerns: Cannot name a single validated pain scale for either dogs or cats
Cannot state any rescue analgesia threshold score
Cannot describe what FAS stands for or that high FAS inflates pain scores

high_value_elements: Asks specific behavioral history questions: Can the cat jump to its usual height? Has litter box entry changed? Has grooming of hard-to-reach areas changed? Has interaction with family decreased?
Applies Feline Grimace Scale during the physical examination (validated for in-hospital use)
Correctly interprets FGS score of 6/10 as above rescue threshold of ≥4; recognizes owner verbal score of 2/10 reflects chronic functional observation (different construct)
Differentiates chronic OA pain from CDS, hyperthyroidism, and normal aging using specific clinical criteria
Establishes documented baseline allowing future comparisons to monitor treatment response
major_concerns: Relies solely on owner description without applying any validated instrument
Interprets owner verbal score as overriding the FGS score without understanding what each measure captures
Attributes all behavioral changes in the geriatric cat to normal aging without structured assessment

high_value_elements: Identifies prey species pain under-recognition as the highest-priority systematic assessment failure: evolutionary hardwiring suppresses pain display to avoid predator detection
Applies minimum post-procedural assessment standard to all prey species regardless of behavioral appearance: fixed time interval assessment using physiological indicators
Correctly interprets discordant physiological indicators (HR 140, non-weight-bearing, anorexia) as evidence of under-treated pain despite low behavioral pain scale score
major_concerns: Cannot identify the under-recognition risk for any prey species
Interprets a quiet, non-vocalizing post-surgical rabbit or bird as necessarily pain-free
Has no strategy for assessing pain in a patient where behavioral scoring is unreliable

high_value_elements: Explains evolutionary basis for pain masking in prey species: mid-level predators that display weakness are targeted by larger predators; behavioral suppression of pain cues is adaptive

Explains why owner-reported and clinic-based scores differ: owners observe habitual behavior over weeks (functional domain); clinicians observe immediate behavioral/facial response to examination (acute domain)
Justifies FGS for caged or hospitalized cats: facial action units are observable without requiring the cat to locomote
Explains why stoic breeds suppress behavioral pain expression; physiological responses are harder to consciously suppress
major_concerns: Cannot explain why prey species mask pain from an evolutionary standpoint
Cannot explain why a discrepant owner and clinic score warrants further investigation

high_value_elements: Acknowledges validated acute pain scales do not exist for all species; best-available adaptations must be used with explicit uncertainty
Acknowledges challenge of distinguishing chronic pain from CDS in geriatric cats and describes multimodal workup and analgesia trial
Identifies when specialist consultation is appropriate for complex neuropathic or exotic species presentations
major_concerns: Claims to accurately assess pain in all species using the same scale developed for dogs or cats
Cannot identify any patient type for which current validated tools are insufficient or absent

high_value_elements: Integrates WASVA pain guidelines: pain is the fourth vital sign; assessment baseline before surgery, every 30 minutes for 6-8 hours post-op; avoid disturbing cats eating or sleeping as signs of adequate pain control

Discusses gabapentin's effect on neurological exam: pre-visit gabapentin in cats affects gait and postural reactions; neurological assessments must be timed to account for half-life (4 hours normal cats; longer in CKD)
Connects assessment accuracy directly to treatment outcome: unmeasured pain is effectively untreated pain; systematic assessment detects and corrects oligoanalgesia
major_concerns: Cannot integrate WASVA guideline recommendations into a clinical workflow
Treats pain assessment as an optional clinical extra rather than a core standard of care
